desc:
The road stretches east and west. To the east, the Bree gate can be spotted.
Westbound lies the trade district of Bree before arriving at central Bree, and
houses line the street to the north and south. The noise and the hustle and
bustle of the city seems to be much quieter here.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The street continues to stretch to the east and the west, packed firm underfoot
by heavy foot and hoof traffic. North lies several houses tightly cramped
together, while to the south the noise of metalworkers can be heard. The Bree
gate can be seen off in the distance to the east.
name: Old East Road

desc:
A wide space opens up before the smith's working area to the south, plenty of
space for wagons both to deliver and haul off weapons, arrows and armour for
buyers. The white sand packed street is divided with poles and ropes to allow a
good flow of visitors to pass the wagons parked.
name: Metalworker's Bend

desc:
The street of white sand and gravel widens towards the south, coming to a large
bend. Houses align the street both to the east and west, with about six yards
between them. A dark and narrow alley winds its way west between some of the
shops, contrasting the bright blue and pretty shop to the east.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The small and dirty path leads around a few corners and ends in front of three
houses. Most windows here are protected by wooden folding shutters, whereas many
doors were repaired more than once with thin beams. A few barrels with rusty
hoops here are obviously used to collect water that drips from rooftops during
rain. The alleyway continues westward.
name: Narrow Alley between Wooden Houses

desc:
Tall stone walls form the perimeter of this wide alleyway. The brick walkway
here is unkempt and uneven, forming a tripping hazard for unwary travellers. The
alley continues to the east, where it constricts as the buildings draw nearer. A
road can be seen to the west.
name: Wide Brick Alleyway

desc:
The stone wall to the west echoes all sounds, down to the most silent whisper.
The road is made from large stones stacked together, with dirt and sand
supporting them. Looking north, Bree-hill stretches out upwards with several
buildings in the far distance. South, the road comes up to a bend.
name: Mason Road

desc:
The stone road bends sharply here, neatly cut between the buildings. To the
east, a tall and dark building looms on the street corner, the chattering of
loud voices heard within. Slightly downhill to the west lies the city cemetery
besides a large wooden building.
name: Mason Road

desc:
A large dock has been built on pilings reaching over the city fishing spot here,
allowing easy access with boat or a fishing rod. On the dock a solid shack has
been built, with thick walls facing west and east while open both north and
south. Inside, tools for the fishing trade are being kept while a fireplace and
a few chairs provide heat and comfort. Clear water laps against the sides of the
dock.
name: Pier upon a Small Pond

desc:
The stone road comes to a wider stretch here, and is now a mixture of smaller
stones among the large stone blocks. A few blocks seems loose on the northern
side, where water seems to have washed away some of the dirt. Just off the road
to the north a large dock sticks out over the water. To the south a triangular
wall dominates the view and not far west the road comes to and end.
name: Mason Road

desc:
Only four large stones stacked together make the width of the road, a narrow
five feet between the building to the north and a stone wall to the south. To
the east the road turns northbound where a large dark building dominates the
corner. Westwards lies the city cemetery and several buildings.
name: Mason Road

desc:
The large stone blocks come to an abrupt end where the road widens into a dirt
packed square. Westwards rises a steep hill where a large green hedge stretches
towards the skies. A small cemetery can be seen over a stone fence, and can be
accessed though a wooden gate.
name: End of Mason Road

desc:
The stone road is wide and dusty, and ruts are etched into the stone, cut there
over time by wagon wheels and animal hooves. To the north, the road winds toward
the heart of the city. To the south, many buildings of different shapes and
sizes dot the landscape, where residents of Bree have made their homes.
name: Mason Road

desc:
Long stones have been embedded in the dirt here reminiscent of a cobblestone
road, though less sophisticated. A low wooden rail about a foot high flanks the
street on either side. The houses here seem to be all made from rough wooden
boards discoloured from the winds and rain.
name: South Mason Road

desc:
A well-crafted stretch of stone road stretches north and south here. Each stone
has been carefully cut, leaving only enough room for a few small weeds to peek
through the gaps. A small dirt path follows the road on either side, evidently
trod down by those not wishing to be run over by wagons and carriages.
name: South Mason Road

desc:
This road is paved with finely cut blocks of grey stone. Shallow trenches. been
dug a few inches deep on either side of the thoroughfare to allow the storm
water to flow away without damaging the street. Some of the slabs are covered
with patches of thick, green moss.
name: South Mason Road

desc:
Moss covers most of the rocks lining this section of the street. The stone-paved
road is cracked and weathered, with small tufts of grass sprouting up in various
spots. To the west, a line of hedges can just be seen between the rows of
houses.
name: South Mason Road

desc:
The road here is built from packed red clay. Wheel tracks have created two ruts
in the dirt making the footing uneven. The walls of the houses bordering the
street are mostly covered in a deep red moss, giving the whole area a desert
feel.
name: Southwind Lane

desc:
A high wooden fence surrounds a few of the houses here. The foot of the fence is
caked with dirt from the road and little bits of greenery can be seen growing on
it. The street consists of packed, reddish-brown clay with sprigs of grass
poking up all along it.
name: Southwind Lane

desc:
Large patches of ivy grow along the sides of the narrow lane. The walls of the
houses bordering the street have long strings of ivy crawling up them. Some
wooden planks have been placed on the ground here and there acting as a solid
place to stand if the road turns to mud.
name: Southwind Lane

desc:
Well-maintained grass stretches about the plaza around a wide stone dais raised
in the centre. The masonry of the dais seems to be fairly rudimentary with many
of the stone blocks being of slightly varying dimensions. Houses surrond the
plaza to the north, west, and south.
name: Highbluff Plaza

desc:
The ground here rises and falls gently. A thick layer of grass and small plants
dominates the area with small patches of dirt appearing here and there. A small
path of bare dirt winds its way between mounds and divots. A small circle of
stones surrounds the charred earth of a camp fire.
name: Hilly Glade

desc:
The inner part of the guards' mess hall is a large kitchen, where food is being
prepared for duty-fulfilling men and women. Several long tables are placed with
benches for seating and all the benches are covered with grey and black wolf
furs. A braided thick rug covers the room in all directions, and the walls are
fully covered with red and golden wool drapes.
name: Mess Hall

desc:
Grimy and dirty, this house stands out in the Bree eastern area. The dark wood
walls, covered in grime and dust, sport no painting or ornament. Tiny ridges
cover the warped wooden floor, the result of ill upkeep. The windows are
similarly grime-covered, and their frames are rotting, crumbling vestiges of
once intricate handiwork.
name: Bill Ferny's House

desc:
With exits both from the west and north, the armourer's large working area is
divided by a path through it. On the northwestern side the tools, benches and
anvils are placed, all worn yet well-kept. A large angular desk is placed to the
southeast, where armour, cloaks and shields for sale are placed and a few
special items are hung for display on the wall. Between the armour a wooden sign
with a notice is hung.
name: Armour Shop

desc:
The dirt is mostly packed flat, except in a few places where it has been dug
into mounds supporting pieces of metal. The metal, some new and fresh and some
remnants of armour and weapons, is being sorted and stacked for use. The
fletcher, armourer, and weaponsmith of Bree all use the metals from here for
their daily work.
name: Metalworker's Square

desc:
Several large anvils are the first things that catch the eyes when entering,
each longer than the smith is tall. The worn metal is gleaming of oil, and in
several places, pieces are missing where a hammer hit wrong. Two stone furnaces
blaze the room with heat, fuelled by air blowing over coal. A long counter
stands across the length of the room, not far from the door, and the weaponsmith
displays his wares safely behind it, perhaps fearing theft.
name: Weapon Shop

desc:
The walls of this brick room are covered in a thick layer of black soot, and the
floor is lined with trails of black ash and silver metal bits. The ceiling is
high here, allowing ample airflow for the smelting of iron and steel. A shop can
be seen to the east.
name: Metalworker's Forge

desc:
The long and narrow interior of this house rises almost two stories tall. Long
sticks, the straightest the Bree saw can deliver, stand tall along the southern
wall. Boxes of arrowheads come straight from the smith to the south, ready to be
made into arrows.
name: Fletcher's Workshop

desc:
A large room opens up, almost square in shape. The walls are all painted in a
bright blue colour, contrasting a red wooden floor. Large hooks on the wall
display various ropes, most used for construction and building work and some
with hooks for climbing. Tools to create ropes lie neatly placed and organised
on workbenches.
name: Rope Carver's Store

desc:
A northern wall in grey stone is contrasting the rest of this large office,
where all other walls are made from finely cut wooden planks painted brightly
yellow. Windows on three sides make it naturally lit while its day, through the
western window a fishing dock can be observed.
name: Bree Official Planning Office

desc:
In a drastic contrast to the dark exterior of this house, the inside is bright
both in colour and lighting. The windows have all been nailed shut from the
inside, with only suspended oil lanterns creating the light. A wide wooden
ladder leads up to a loft above, and a sign points down towards a gambling room
though a wooden cellarhatch.
name: Freebooters' Society

desc:
This simple room is walled with thick slabs of pine, and the rafters of its
sloped roof are exposed, giving the room a rustic feel. The floorboars are
similarly composed of pine, and are wide, smooth and without dimples or ridges.
The austere abode is heated warmly by a fireplace.
name: Clint's Small House

desc:
A damp floor of packed dirt holds several cots and bunk beds, with grass packed
mattresses for sleeping. The walls and roof seem solid, made out of different
wood, timber and a few stones - clearly built from leftovers. A few torches
throw a dim but dancing light, and a fireplace near the entrance warms anyone
living here.
name: Poor House

desc:
Beneath a steep hill both to the west and south, memorial plaques and stones
have been placed, shaded by the Bree hedge growing above. Pathways of white
gravel marbled with grey and black pass though the well tended grassy area. In
several places, flowers and lights have been put down in someone's memory.
name: Well-tended City Cemetery

desc:
Both northern and southern walls are triangular, meeting at a high peak almost
twenty feet up. All walls have been coloured green to about a man's height and
white to the top. Two massive chandeliers are suspended from the ceiling, with
large white candles lighting the room and two solid iron candelabrums on each
side of a large desk.
name: Seer's Lodging

desc:
This oblong loft space has been decorated like a narrow street, being lit by a
lamp that looks like any street-lamp found in Bree. Mannequins representing
citizens and other travellers have been equipped with purses, handbags and other
resembling items that can be stolen.
name: Freebooters' Training Loft

desc:
A clear sign of dwarven masonry is seen on several of the stone blocks making up
the walls in this cellar, being carved with intricate scenes from battles. The
air here is thick with excitement and nervousness. It is not always large
amounts of money that are at stake; some people pay their gambling addiction
with their lives.
name: Gambling Basement

desc:
The ground is strewn with a white-grey gravel that crunches underfoot. In one
corner of the square, some holes have been dug into the ground where a wide
hitching post stands a few feet above the ground. The footprints of horses
litter the ground.
name: Western Square

desc:
The grass stops abruptly and turns into mud as it approaches the pond. The water
itself is relatively clear, though the muddy surface beneath gives it a brown
tint. A shoddy wooden pier juts out into the water almost reaching the centre of
the pond.
name: Small Pond

desc:
A warm glow spills from the windows of the Clarkston Inn, beckoning travelers
from the cobblestone streets of Bree. Above the stable adjacent to the entrance,
a weathered sign swings gently, its painted letters proclaiming hospitality
within. The scent of hay and the soft nickering of horses fill the stable area.
To the east, the murmur of a fountain can be discerned, while the west holds the
promise of the inn's cozy confines.
name: Clarkston Inn Stables

desc:
Only about four feet in height and width near the entrance from above, growing
larger towards the eastern end this stone crypt holds shelves on any available
surface. They are filled with small vials, flasks and trinkets, most of these
covered with a thick layer of dust.
name: Dimly Lit Stone Crypt

desc:
A hushed tone pervades this cramped hall under the eaves of southern Bree's
foundation. The low room has stone-laid recesses set into the walls. In these
lie the decayed remains of the ancient Edain of many millennia ago. Ancient
writings in a dialect of Westron describe the occupants, but the precise
meanings cannot be gleaned, so changed is the modern language from its forebear.
name: Quiet Resting Place

desc:
Hardly any sunlight is being admitted into this small and dank room. The windows
on the southern side have been draped with heavy curtains, disallowing the
passer-by from Highbluff Plaza even a glimpse from the interior. The wooden
walls have been decorated with numerous paintings of coins, jewellery, weapons
and artefacts.
name: Scylhiun's Shelter

desc:
The room is a harmonious blend of elegance and function, with walls painted in
soothing hues reminiscent of the tranquil waters of Lake Linaewen. An ample bed
sits adjacent to a large desk, where meticulously organized vials and healing
herbs suggest preparations for tending to the wounded and weary. Above the desk,
a windows are positioned to catch the light of Earendil, ensuring the room is
bathed in the gentle radiance that aids Melchor's healing arts.
name: Healer's Refuge

desc:
The chamber is serene, with walls paneled in dark wood and a window that frames
a view of the starlit sky, a mirror of the forest canopies under which he was
raised. An elegant, slender sword rests above the headboard of a gracefully
carved bed, the linen fine and cool to the touch. A faint scent of pine and
earth lingers, complementing the elf's martial grace with the tranquil essence
of the woodland realm.
name: Xile's Sanctuary

desc:
A stunning staircase leads into the chamber below, seemingly made of the purest
of white marble. It apparently is of dwarven make. A crystal chandelier hangs
from the ceiling, while several trophies and various gleaming weapons of war
hang along the walls.
name: On a Descending Staircase

desc:
This vast cavern stretches several feet in every direction. The only exit is the
spiral stone stair case, rising up from the center of this circular underground
room. the surface of the floor, walls, and ceiling are covered in a glossy
smooth black marble. The craftsmanship is of such quality that the transitions
of the surfaces are indiscernible, an obvious sign that the stonework was
crafted by a master mason. Set in the floor at the bottom of the staircase is a
large mithril mural depicting a hammer crowned by 3 stars. Behind the staircase
is a four-poster bed composed of the bedrock itself, and topped by a thick
feather mattress. The posts stretch all the way to the ceiling, and carved into
them are many intricate designs of rose vines. These designs are likewise
comprised of mithril, embedded into the stone of the posts. The stone of the bed
is likewise covered with the same seamless black marble that covers the surfaces
in the rest of the room. Several Bookcases line the walls, and a large fireplace
is set into the wall at the foot of the bed.
name: Black Marble Cavern

desc:
This single-room home is very small, humble, and modest. A small stove has been
placed near the western wall, and a small round table is near it, with a single,
wooden chair neatly pushed in underneath it. A small bed has been pushed against
the northern wall, and it has been neatly and tightly made. Everything is tidy
and orderly, with nothing noticeably out of place. Elven tapestries and artwork
line each wall. A tall, wooden shelf stands alone against the southern wall,
displaying several different Elven trinkets, tomes and curios.
name: Lhu's Humble Home

desc:
Within the minstrel's dwelling, an oak harp, finely carved with intricate horse
motifs, stands beside a weathered leather-bound book of ancient ballads. Hanging
on the stone walls, banners embroidered with tales of valour flutter gently in
the breeze from the open window. A sturdy wooden table bears scrolls of
parchment filled with lyrical compositions, while polished silver goblets gleam
alongside a well-used quill and inkwell. In a corner, a woven tapestry depicts a
moonlit plains where horses dance to the melody of a forgotten song, its notes
seemingly alive in the soft glow of the hearth fire.
name: Harpstone Haven

desc:
Straight beams of cut timber make up the structure of this simple cottage. A
large bed, with four wooden posts topped with carven horse-heads, sits in one
corner. The walls are unadorned, save a large tapestry of armoured riders
crossing a field of flowing green grass. A circular rug covers the floor. It is
woven with an emblem of a shield resting on a pair crossed spears.
name: Timber-framed Cottage

desc:
The boulder-strewn hilltops' surface is pocked with moss-filled nooks and
crannies. Within the cracks of the boulders, small colonies of lichens thrive.
The boulders form what appears to be a crude circle, suggestive of an ancient
meeting place. Stonecrop clings stubbornly to the rocky outcrops, their yellow
blossoms adding a dash of colour to the otherwise grey tableau. The west is
marked by a range of hills that roll across the horizon. To the north, a
solitary hillock rises, emerald green and studded with grey stones.
name: Boulder-covered Hilltops

desc:
Within the hill, a burrow reveals itself, nestled amidst the boulders and
earthen walls. The air in this cavern is oppressively humid, and every surface
is dangerously slick. The rocks are covered in moss, a few bare patches reveal
that the cave walls are made of granite. Roots protrude down through the cavern
ceiling. The cave's exit to the west, concealed by natural formations, leads
into the hills.
name: Inside the Hill

desc:
Along the path, the traveller's gaze is drawn to the fir trees, few but fair,
their verdant spires standing tall against the pastel panorama. Limestone
outcrops, ancient bones of the earth, jut forth from the hillsides. To the west
and east, the hills rise sharply, their steep sides veiled with a mixture of
moss and creeping ivy, a vibrant green tapestry. The trail narrows as it enters
a meandering maze of rolling emerald hills to the north. To the south, the trail
continues into a shallow valley.
name: Winding Trail through the Hillocks

desc:
A turning trail cuts through the verdant canvas, winding its way across the
undulating South Downs landscape. Next to this trail, a huddle of wild marjoram
makes its home, rooted in the heart of a limestone crevice. The trail pushes
onward, its path set to the west and north, further into the heart of the hills.
To the east and south, the hills rise sharply. Their slopes are steep and
treacherous, a deterrent to all but the hardiest of adventurers.
name: Turning Trail across the Hills

desc:
The entrance to the cave in the west is veiled by a curtain of verdant ivy and
moss. Piles of bones lie scattered all over this small cave. Well-trampled bear
droppings and dirt cover the cave floor which contribute to the repulsive musty
odour. It is evident that in this rugged refuge, a mighty creature seeks solace
and protection.
name: Bear's Den in the Hills

desc:
The hills of the South Downs have broken away to the northeast and southwest to
reveal a protected prairie. Only slight undulations disrupt an otherwise smooth
area of land. Some burrows are tucked away against the sheltered edges of hills
to the north. Imprints of larger animals indicate a favored route of wild
creatures.
name: Peaceful Field

desc:
A large portion of the ground shows evidence of having been plowed in the past
but never followed through on planting. A small, dilapidated shack nearby
indicates that this was once meant to be farmland but has been abandoned. The
shack only has one wall standing with a jumble of half-burnt wood making up the
rest of the debris.
name: Plowed Field

desc:
The area is covered in knee-high bushes. They are remarkably stiff and make
movement difficult as the branches tug and resist shifting. The ground between
bushes is sparse grass mixed with broken twigs and dirt. Some large boulders
partially obscure a cave in the hill to the west.
name: Short Brush

desc:
This hill is clearly part of the South Downs extending to the northwest and
southeast, but is separated by steep edges to the west and east. Exposed rocks
and dirt tumble precipitously from the top of the hill. A patch of brush extends
to the north.
name: Isolated Mound

desc:
Thick grass grows tall and droops back toward the ground. The vegetation covers
the area completely and appears more pale than the adjoining meadow. The lush
green colour is replaced with something closer to white and appears almost
sickly. A large growth of brush extends to the south.
name: Pale Field

desc:
Through the dense, creeping plants covering the ground, there is faint evidence
of a path that runs north to south. The path has not been maintained or used
regularly, and as a result, plants have overgrown it, making it difficult to see
and use. It is unclear where the path leads, but it is a reminder of a time when
this area was more heavily travelled.
name: Overgrown Path

desc:
The centre of the area was once a crossroads where paths trail off to the north
and south. Fainter tracks split off to the west and east but it isn't clear if
they were created by animals or human inhabitants. Bushes and creeping vines
have enveloped the landscape.
name: Overgrown Crossing

desc:
A long abandoned trail snakes between the north and south. Grass covering the
hills to the south covers the trail as it climbs in that direction. Dense vines
prevent grass from growing over the old path as it moves to the north. Leafy
climbing plants resist any movement.
name: Faint Path in the Brush

desc:
Tall, leafy plants from the north have begun invading the grassy hills in the
area. The South Downs extend to the west and south. Some established animal
paths coming from the north have beaten down the grass in narrow slits winding
through the hills.
name: South Downs

desc:
The South Downs hills are smooth and grassy, dominating the landscape. Scattered
trees dot the area, but none grow close enough to form a grove. To the west, a
deep trench prevents movement. The trench is filled with dirt and a few decaying
sticks.
name: South Downs

desc:
Grass of varying length spreads over the area. Some areas are very short, having
been nibbled down nearly to the dirt by the roving animals. Other sections are
frequented less and allowed the vegetation to grow higher. Many hoof prints are
stamped into the ground. A wagon track leads east to a barn.
name: Peaceful Pasture

desc:
This is a very large barn with numerous stalls for animals, hay, and grain
storage. One stall holds barrels of wine while others are reserved for
livestock. Some equipment stands in the corner for processing grapes before
being barrelled. The farm house stands to the east while vineyards and pasture
flow to the south and west.
name: Large Barn

desc:
Long rows of grapevines stretch out north to south. The vines are pruned and
well-kept. Thick brown stalks snake horizontally above the ground with smaller
green vines growing up and over a rope. Billowy green leaves mingle with heavy
bunches of grapes. Soft, short grass covers the fertile soil between rows. A
fence protects the space to the east and south.
name: Vibrant Vineyard

desc:
The sprawling fields of crops are dominated by wheat, with smaller areas of
corn, hay, and oats. Fertile ground has contributed to impressive crop heights.
A garden has been carved out of the field to serve the farm workers. An ageing,
wooden fence encloses the area to the west and south.
name: Wheat Fields

desc:
The inside of the farmhouse is spacious. The front door opens to a crackling
fire warming the living room. To the left is a long hallway with several living
quarters for farmhands. To the right is a bright kitchen filled with ripe
vegetables from the garden and freshly-baked bread.
name: Expansive Farmhouse

desc:
This tunnel appears man-made with walls having clear marks from picks and
shovels. The floor is plain dirt, and the ceiling is supported by beams and
rafters. Some larger stones, apparently left over from the excavation, have been
pushed against the walls. A ladder leads up to a trap door in the ceiling.
name: Tunnel End

desc:
This large, carved-out chamber could comfortably house several people. Several
cots are arranged around a central fire, and cooking equipment is scattered
nearby. The air is thick with smoke and filth, and a pile of trash and debris
has been heaped against the south wall. Torches hang from the walls.
name: Living Chamber

desc:
This small room appears to be the storage room for smoked meats, berries and
several barrels of beer. Some cooking utensils and spare equipment lie in the
dirt. The walls have the familiar cuts from human workmanship. The ceiling
supports here look the least stable in the tunnels.
name: Cluttered Storeroom

desc:
The ceiling is taller than the tunnel is wide. Some rough beams and stones help
support the ceiling and walls but their placement appears reactionary rather
than as sophisticated architecture. To the south is a messy living area. The
tunnel slopes down towards the north where some dark water covers the floor.
name: Sloping Tunnel

desc:
The chamber is quite cramped and appears carved out over a large stone as the
cool rock makes up the floor of this space. The dirt is dry and dusty,
collecting in soft piles as it is brushed from the walls. The roof of the tunnel
has a slight arch.
name: Dark Tunnel

desc:
The tunnel is completely filled with water. The tunnel walls visible above the
water are smooth for a couple inches and then show shovel and pick marks from
manual excavation. The water is very clear, revealing dark green algae growing
under the surface.
name: Watery Tunnel

desc:
The tunnel bends in a smooth arc to the west and north here. The jagged ceiling
is quite low in some areas leaving less than a yard between the water and the
rocks above. Algae softens the surfaces beneath the surface of the water in some
places.
name: Waterlogged Tunnel Bend

desc:
The tunnel widens here compared to the narrower sections to the north and south.
The widest part is around seven yards from wall to wall. Under the clear water
the bottom of the tunnel is covered in a soft, powdery mud. When disturbed, the
dirt particles slowly drift back down to rest on the tunnel floor.
name: Watery Passage

desc:
The water-filled tunnel continues to the north and south. The tunnel wall opens
to the west into what appears to be a room large enough to stand up in. The wall
to the east may have once also led to another room but is now boarded up with
old-looking wood.
name: Watery Junction

desc:
The tunnel ends here in a larger section of open space. The ceiling is higher
than in other parts of the watery tube although it appears entirely natural
without markings from digging equipment. There is a small hole in the eastern
wall on level with the surface of the water which extends into darkness.
name: Watery Chamber

desc:
This chamber appears to have been a temple. A large arrangement of candles crowd
on the floor around a tall altar. Piles of melted wax shroud whatever implements
hold the unlit candlesticks upright on the floor. The altar is jet black stone
and is covered in elaborate and delicate runes. A thick, putrid smell hangs in
the air.
name: Decrepit Temple

desc:
The tunnel is narrow and winding. It weaves back and forth as if avoiding
obstacles hidden behind the earthen walls. Empty torch holders are attached to
the walls at regular intervals. The tunnel leads to an open room to the north
and an ordinary metal door at the south west end.
name: Musty Corridor

desc:
The large cavern is mostly empty save for some broken wooden crates and other
debris. Some of the items may have been clothing or eating equipment but has
degraded beyond identification. Several small tunnel openings line the walls but
only one to the west could potentially be entered.
name: Damp Cave

desc:
Travel is difficult in this small tunnel. It is impossible to avoid scraping
against the stony ceiling and walls. The tunnel is winding and weaves in several
directions but generally runs east and west. There are some small bones
scattered in the dirt.
name: Narrow Tunnel

desc:
This small chamber is tall enough to stand up in and appears to be a large nest
for rodents. A pile of things they have pilfered from the inhabited sections of
the cave system stands in a heap mixed with feces. What was once a cloak lies in
shreds and coiled up as a bed for the rodents.
name: Small Chamber in the Rat Tunnels

desc:
The floor of the cave is a solid, mostly flat piece of rock. It is clean without
any evidence of dirt or leaves left to clutter the room. While it appears this
cave would be an ideal home for critters seeking a hidden home, there is no
indication that animals frequent the spot.
name: Small Cave

desc:
Some worn bones have been collected into a corner of this cave. It appears to
have been used by a bear or other wild animals at some point but has since seen
further excavation to expand the area shown by a stark contrast in wall textures
as the cave moves deeper into the hill. Tin and iron cooking equipment is
scattered around a fire pit with animal furs in a pile nearby.
name: Abandoned Bear Den

desc:
This barn lies in slumber, the roof is sagging and once-sturdy timbers on the
walls are now weathered and worn. The wooden dividers of abandoned stalls are
worn and splintered, and the hay that once cushioned the feet of horses is now
reduced to dust. Cobwebs hang like delicate shrouds in the rafters above. The
barn continues to the north. To the east, lies the front door to the farmhouse.
To the west, a strong wooden gate stands.
name: Dusty Barn

desc:
The air within this abandoned cattle shed is still and heavy, laden with the
scent of dust. The floor of the shed is covered in a layer of dust and the
interior of the barn is shrouded in shadow. In the rafters above, the remnants
of old lanterns hang, their glass panes cracked and clouded, their flames long
extinguished. The door to the north, heavy and creaking, hangs askew from its
hinges. The barn continues to the east and south.
name: Rustic Cattle Shed

desc:
This ramshackle structure, with its timbers worn, barely manages to hold itself
together. The scent of rotten hay mingles with the stench of faeces. In the dim
corners, the rotten corpses of cattle lay forgotten, their eyes wide and
unseeing. The earth beneath the feet is uneven, trodden by countless hooves that
have long since departed. A backdoor to the north with deep cracks reveals a
grassy grazing field. The barn continues to the west.
name: Forlorn Barn

desc:
This serene landscape, nestled between the steep, impassable hills of the South
Downs to the north and east, offers a stark contrast to the decaying barn to the
south, its timbers worn and weathered. A pond lies nestled in the heart of the
field, its waters calm and still. At the edge of the pond, there is a grove of
ancient willow trees, their branches bending low. To the west, a field of crops
dances gracefully in the breeze. To the south of this field, is a backdoor into
the barn.
name: Grassy Grazing Field by a Pond

desc:
The earth beneath the crops is rich and dark, teeming with life as worms and
insects work tirelessly to enrich the soil. The ground itself, though fertile,
is somewhat marshy, springing slightly beneath each step as the water that
sustains the land mingles with the earth. To the west, the hills ascend so
steeply that no traveller could hope to venture upwards. To the south lies a
rustic barn, with its weathered wood and thatched roof.
name: Field of Barley

desc:
The field stretches to the south, with a vast expanse of swaying stalks. Amid
this field stands a single, ancient tree, the bark of this venerable sentinel is
gnarled and weathered, bearing the scars of many years. The South Downs to the
west, north, and east form a natural barrier. Venturing farther south, the
ground beneath grows softer, more pliant. The once-firm soil gives way to a
marshier terrain, with the earth taking on a darker hue.
name: Field of Wheat

desc:
The walls of this quaint farmhouse are built from the stones gathered from the
surrounding hills. In the centre of the room, an old, round table serves as the
heart of the home, surrounded by chairs with hand-carved backs depicting the
bountiful harvests that have fed the family for generations. A sturdy bed made
of oak, adorned with intricately carved floral patterns. A set of rustic
cupboards stands against the wall. To the west, a door of heavy oak opens to
reveal the barn.
name: Rustic Farmhouse

desc:
The tavern is dimly lit and very cosy, with a bar that was carefully carved out
of an unique marble that stands along the northern wall. There are several clean
glasses neatly stacked behind the bar with many glass bottles of various
coloured liquors. In the centre of the room is a low, wide table surrounded by
several overstuffed armchairs that look very inviting to any weary traveller.
There is a large fireplace along the eastern wall with a dark, wooden mantle
above it. Small windows are on both the western and eastern walls, which allows
the sunlight to slightly beam in during the day. A statue of Almarian stands
beneath the windows along the western wall.
name: Dwarven Tavern

desc:
This is a small, cosy space carved almost entirely out of grey stone. There is a
large stone fireplace on the eastern wall with a large, black bearskin rug lying
on the floor in front of it. There is a huge, wooden-framed bed that seems to
take up most of the room. There are no windows, but there are small sconces that
have candles within them, adorning the stone walls for decor and giving a faint
light throughout the bedroom.
name: Fain's Bedroom

desc:
The first main room of the Clarkston Inn envelops visitors in the rich, smoky
aroma of barbecue, a testament to the inn's hearty fare. Heavy wooden beams
stretch across the ceiling, and a large fireplace casts a warm glow on the
well-worn tables and chairs. Toward the east, the stable's presence is a subtle
undertone to the more enticing scents of the kitchen.
name: Clarkston Inn

desc:
The narrow hallway runs westward, its walls adorned with tapestries depicting
scenes of Middle-earth's storied past. The planks beneath whisper of countless
footsteps that have traversed this corridor. Doors flank the hall to the north
and south, each carved with intricate patterns and promising rest and respite
within. Soft light filters through oil lamps, casting a warm, golden hue that
dances across the wood.
name: Decorated Hallway

desc:
In the back of the Clarkston Inn lies its kitchen, a functional space combining
rustic aesthetics with the buzz of activity. High ceilings with wooden beams
frame a room flooded with natural light from a large window, highlighting the
clean, well-used surfaces. The centerpiece is a large island, surrounded by
copper pots and pans hanging above, and near the window, fresh herbs dry on a
rack, adding a subtle aroma to the air. A stone hearth at one end of the kitchen
provides heat and a cooking area, next to which stands a chopping block with a
collection of sharp knives.
name: Stocked Kitchen

desc:
The room includes a fancy wooden bed and a robust desk for armor maintenance and
strategic planning. Muted gray walls are adorned with select pieces of armor and
a rack holding an assortment of swords. A lone window faces the inn?s courtyard,
allowing natural light to brighten the well-worn floorboards.
name: Guts' Garrison

desc:
The room shimmers with a faint, ethereal light, indicating the chamber of
Ailwon, the elven mage. Upon entry, the air seems to hum with a latent power,
and the room appears suffused with a soft, silver glow, akin to moonlight
filtering through forest leaves. The furnishings are sparse but elegant, with a
slender bed carved from pale wood and draped with gossamer linens that whisper
of Elven realms. Shelves bearing scrolls and ancient tomes line the walls, and a
delicate crystal phial, filled with a star-like radiance, serves as a solitary
beacon in the quiet of the room.
name: Ailwon's Haven

desc:
North of the main room, through a sturdy door carved with the visage of a bear,
lies the chamber reserved for Brogan the Beorning. Inside, the furnishings are
robust and unadorned, echoing the simplicity and strength of Beorn's folk. A
large, fur-covered bed dominates the space, providing ample comfort for a man of
considerable size. The walls are hung with weapons and shields, and a single
window offers a view of the distant Misty Mountains, a silent call to the wild
from whence he came.
name: Brogan's Rest

desc:
Beneath an inn, in a room crafted from the surrounding earth, a dwarf
meticulously plans a daring endeavor from his stone-walled chamber. The space,
equipped with a single lantern for light, is filled with ancient maps and
detailed plans of Moria, spread across a well-worn oak table surrounded by tools
and bits of food. Weapons and armor of dwarven craftsmanship are neatly stored
in the room, hinting at the preparation for battle. This secluded room, marked
by its practical setup and strategic importance, serves as Burk's headquarters
for plotting the retaking of Moria and confronting the Balrog that resides
within.
name: Dwarven Command Cave

desc:
Right beside the Clarkston Inn's main kitchen, there's a small, hobbit-sized
cooking nook. It mirrors the larger kitchen's rustic style but scaled down, with
a central table and shelves stocked with tiny utensils and pots. Despite the
bustling activity next door, this nook has a quiet charm, highlighted by two odd
rocks sitting unused on the counter, adding a touch of mystery amidst the array
of miniature cooking tools and ingredients. This space serves as a bridge
between the inn's culinary operations and the unique, homely flair of hobbit
cuisine, with every detail tailored to the hobbit cook's needs.
name: Chef's Quarters

desc:
The walls are lined with shelves, each laden with relics and tomes of Dwarven
lore, their spines embossed with runes that speak of ancient wisdom and
devotion. At the center, a sturdy bed is flanked by a stone altar, upon which a
few modest candles burn steadily, their flames reflecting off polished
ceremonial symbols. The air is heavy with incense, mingling with the
ever-present scent of the mountain stone that seems to resonate with the dwarf's
own steadfast nature.
name: Cleric's Keep

desc:
The room is a bastion of dwarven might; the stone flooring is reminiscent of the
halls of Erebor, and the heavy bed is built to endure the weight of armor and
warrior alike. Weapons are proudly displayed on the wall, a silent testament to
battles fought and foes vanquished. The air is thick with the scent of iron and
leather, and the muted echo of distant forges seems to resonate in the very
stones of the room, as if calling Burk to the honor of his kin.
name: Warrior's Hold
